::: content-hidden \newcommand{\deriv}[2]{\frac{\rd #1}{\rd #2}} \newcommand{\nthderiv}[3]{\frac{\rd^#3 #1}{\rd #2}} \newcommand{\R}{\mathbb R} \newcommand\norm[1]{\left\lVert#1\right\rVert} \newcommand{\bfx}{\mathbf x} \newcommand\mbf[1]{\textbf{#1}} \newcommand\ttt[1]{\texttt{#1}} \newcommand\flo{\text{fl}} \newcommand{\green}[1]{\color{green}{#1}} \newcommand{\red}[1]{\color{red}{#1}} \newcommand{\blue}[1]{\color{blue}{#1}} \newcommand{\black}[1]{\color{black}{#1}} ::: ## Lecture 4 {.unnumbered .unlisted} ::: {style="font-size: 100%;"} [**Learning objectives:**]{style="color:red;"} Solve systems linear equations using Direct Methods - **Gaussian elimination with back substitution** - Operation counts (complexity) ::: {.fragment .fade-in}
[**Motivation:**]{style="color:red;"} Numerically solve systems of $n$ equations in $n$ variables of the form $$ \begin{aligned} E_{1}: \quad& a_{11}\color{red}{x_{1}}\black{+a_{12}}\red{x_{2}}\black{+\dots+a_{1n}}\red{x_{n}}\black{=}\blue{b_{1}}&\\ E_{2}: \quad& a_{21}\red{x_{1}}\black{+a_{22}}\red{x_{2}}\black{+\dots+a_{2n}}\red{x_{n}}\black{=}\blue{b_{2}}&\\ \vdots\quad& \qquad\vdots&\\ E_{n}: \quad& a_{n1}\red{x_{1}}\black{+a_{n2}}\red{x_{2}}\black{+\dots+a_{nn}}\red{x_{n}}\black{=}\blue{b_{n}} \end{aligned} \nonumber $$ Given the constants $\{a_{ij}\}_{i,j=1}^{n}$ and $\blue{\{b_{i}\}_{i=1}^{n}}$ the goal is to compute the independent variables $\red{x_{1},\dots,x_{n}}$. ::: ::: ### {.unnumbered .unlisted} The system above can be written in matrix-vector form as $A \mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ where $$ \overbrace{\begin{bmatrix} a_{11}& a_{12} &\dots& a_{1n}\\ a_{21} &a_{22}& \dots& a_{2n}\\ \vdots& \vdots &\ddots&\vdots\\ a_{n1} &\dots&\dots&a_{nn}\\ \end{bmatrix}}^{A}\red{\overbrace{\begin{bmatrix} x_1\\x_{2}\\\vdots \\x_{n}\end{bmatrix}}^{\mathbf{x}}}\black{=}\blue{\overbrace{\begin{bmatrix} b_1\\ b_{2}\\\vdots \\ b_{n}\end{bmatrix}}^{\mathbf{b}}} \nonumber $$ **Direct Methods** are aimed at computing the exact solution of linear systems in a **finite** number of steps. # Direct Methods ## Gaussian Elimination with backward substitution. ### Results from Year 1 {.unnumbered .unlisted} ::: {#nte-nonsingular .callout-note} ::: extrapad 1. A square matrix is (i.e. $n\times n$) is called non-singular if its inverse (denoted by $A^{-1}$) exists. Note that $AA^{-1}=A^{-1}A=I$ where $I$ is the identity matrix. $$ I=\begin{bmatrix} 1 &0&\dots &0\\ 0&1&0\dots& 0\\ \vdots& &\ddots& \vdots\\ 0&0&\cdots&1 \end{bmatrix} \nonumber $$ 2. A square matrix $A$ is non-singular if an only if its determinant (denoted by $\text{det}(A)$) is not zero 3. $A$ is non-singular if and only if the system $A\mbf{x}=\mbf{b}$ has a unique solution for any $\mbf{b}$. ::: ::: ### Motivation - Consider the system of linear equations: $$ \begin{aligned} E_{1}:&\quad 2\,\red{x_{1}}\black{+3}\,\red{x_{2}}\black{+4}\,\red{x_{3}} &=&~\blue{2}\\ E_{2}:&\quad 4\,\red{x_{1}}\black{+9}\,\red{x_{2}}\black{+10}\,\red{x_{3}} &=&~\blue{6}\\ E_{3}:&\quad 6\,\red{x_{1}}\black{+15}\,\red{x_{2}}\black{+20}\,\red{x_{3}} &=&~\blue{12} \end{aligned} \nonumber $$ - can be written in matrix-vector form $A\mbf{x}=\mbf{b}$: $$ \overbrace{\begin{bmatrix} 2& 3 &4\\ 4 &9&10\\ 6 &15&20\\ \end{bmatrix}}^{A}\red{\overbrace{\begin{bmatrix} x_1\\x_{2}\\x_{3}\end{bmatrix}}^{\mathbf{x}}}\black{=}\blue{\overbrace{\begin{bmatrix} 2\\ 6 \\ 12\end{bmatrix}}^{\mathbf{b}} }\nonumber $$ - From core module: - Row operations on $A$ and $\mbf{b}$: - Transform the system into a (reduced) form that can be easily solved. ### {.unnumbered .unlisted} - Augmented system: $\tilde{A}=[A\, |\, \mathbf{b}]=\left[ \begin{array}{ccc|c} 2& 3 &4 &2\\4 &9&10 &6\\6 &15&20 & 12 \end{array}\right]$ - Operations on the rows (denoted by $E_{1}$,$E_{2}$ and $E_{3}$) of $\tilde{A}$ - Obtain reduced form of the system which has zeros below the diagonal: ![](ForFigs/DM1.svg){fig-alt="Performing row operations." fig-align="left" width="100%"} $$ \begin{aligned} E_{2}\to &E_{2}-(a_{21}/a_{11})E_{1}=\overbrace{[4 \quad9\quad 10\quad 6]}^{E_{2}}-(4/2)\overbrace{[2 \quad 3\quad 4\quad 2]}^{E_1}= [0 \quad 3\quad 2\quad 2]\\ E_{3}\to &E_{3}-(a_{31}/a_{11})E_{1}=\overbrace{[6 \quad15\quad 20\quad 12]}^{E_3}-(6/2)\overbrace{[2 \quad 3\quad 4\quad 2]}^{1}= [0 \quad 6\quad 8\quad 6] \end{aligned}\nonumber $$ ### {.unnumbered .unlisted} ![](ForFigs/DM1.svg){fig-alt="Performing row operations." fig-align="left" width="100%"} $$ E_{3}\to E_{3}-(a_{32}/a_{22})E_{2}=\overbrace{[0 \quad 6\quad 8\quad 6]}^{E_3}-(6/3)\overbrace{[0 \quad 3\quad 2\quad 2]}^{E_{2}}= [0 \quad 0\quad 4\quad 2]\nonumber $$ - Note the two steps that we applied above can be written as $$ E_{j}\to E_{j}-(a_{ji}/a_{ii})E_{i},\qquad \text{for}\quad j=i+1,\dots, n \nonumber $$ for $i=1, 2$ and $n=3$. - At each step of the process, the diagonal term $a_{ii}$, below which we aim to create zeros, is referred to as **pivot element**. ### {#sec-gauss1 .unnumbered .unlisted .scrollable} - Row operations do not change the solution to the original system - We can find the solution by solving the equivalent **upper triangular** system: $$ \begin{aligned} 2x_{1}+3x_{2}+4x_{3} & =2\\ +3x_{2}+2x_{3} & =2\\ 4x_{3} & =2 \end{aligned} \nonumber $$ - solve via **backward substitution:** $$ \begin{aligned} x_{3}&=\frac{2}{4}=\frac{1}{2}\\ x_{2}&=\frac{1}{3}\Big(2-2x_{3}\Big)=\frac{1}{3}(2-1)=\frac{1}{3}\\ x_{1}&=\frac{1}{2}\Big(2-4x_{3}-3x_{2} \Big)=\frac{1}{2}\Big(2-\frac{4}{2}-\frac{3}{3}\Big)=-\frac{1}{2} \end{aligned} \Longrightarrow \mbf{x}=\begin{bmatrix}-1/2\\1/3\\1/2\end{bmatrix}\nonumber $$ - Combined procedure: **Gaussian elimination with backward substitution**. [[back to code](#sec-gauss-code)]{.button} ### Numpy arrays {.unlisted .unnumebred .scrollable} ``` {pyodide-python} import numpy as np A=np.array([[2,3,4],[4,9,10],[6,15,20]], dtype=float) b=np.array([[2],[6],[12]], dtype=float) x=np.array([[-1/2],[1/3],[1/2]], dtype=float) print(f"A times x =\n {A@x}") print(f"A times x -b =\n {A@x-b}") tildeA=np.hstack((A,b)) ``` ### Gaussian elimination - We now derive this algorithm for the general system given of $n$ eqs: $$ \overbrace{\begin{bmatrix} a_{11}& a_{12} &\dots& a_{1n}\\ a_{21} &a_{22}& \dots& a_{2n}\\ \vdots& \vdots &\ddots&\vdots\\ a_{n1} &\dots&\dots&a_{nn}\\ \end{bmatrix}}^{A}\overbrace{\begin{bmatrix} x_1\\x_{2}\\\vdots \\x_{n}\end{bmatrix}}^{\mathbf{x}}=\overbrace{\begin{bmatrix} b_1\\ b_{2}\\\vdots \\ b_{n}\end{bmatrix}}^{\mathbf{b}} \qquad \tilde{A}= \left[\begin{array}{cccc|c} a_{11}& a_{12} &\dots& a_{1n}&a_{1,n+1}\\ a_{21} &a_{22}& \dots& a_{2n}&a_{2,n+1}\\ \vdots& \vdots &\ddots&\ddots&\vdots\\ a_{n1} &\dots&\dots&a_{nn}&a_{n,n+1} \end{array}\right]\nonumber $$ ::: extrapad - Start with the augmented matrix $\tilde A=[A\,\vert\, \mathbf{b}]$, - Notation: relabel the entries of $\mbf{b}$ by defining $a_{i,n+1}\equiv b_{i}$ for $i=1,\dots n$. - Entries of $\tilde{A}$ are $[\tilde{A}]_{i,j}=a_{ij}$ for $1\leq i\leq n$, $1\leq j\leq n+1$. ::: ### {.unnumbered .unlisted} - [**First step**]{.alert} of Gaussian elimination: transform original matrix $\tilde{A}^{(1)}\equiv \tilde{A}$ into $\tilde{A}^{(2)}$ with zeros below the first entry of $E_{1}$. - This requires: $E_{j}-(a_{j1}^{(1)}/a_{11}^{(1)})E_{1}\to E_{j}$, for $j= 2,3,\dots,n$. ![](Figures/DM20.svg){fig-alt="First steps of Gaussian Elimination." fig-align="left" width="100%"} - We have included the superscript $^{(1)}$ in all elements of $\tilde{A}^{(1)}$. - Entries from $E_{2},\dots, E_{n}$ in $\tilde{A}^{(2)}$ are different from those in $\tilde{A}^{(1)}$ (hence the superscript $^{(2)}$). ### {.unnumbered .unlisted} - Repeat: transform $\tilde{A}^{(2)}$ to $\tilde{A}^{(3)}$ with zeros below the entry $a_{22}^{(2)}$. - Requires: $E_{j}-(a_{j2}^{(2)}/a_{22}^{(2)})E_{2}\to E_{j}\qquad j= 3,4,\dots n.$ ![](Figures/DM21.svg){fig-alt="First steps of Gaussian Elimination." fig-align="left" width="100%"} ### ith step {.unnumbered .unlisted} ![](ForFigs/LUB_new2B.png){width="100%"} We need to do $E_{j}\to E_{j}-(a_{ji}^{(i)}/a_{ii}^{(i)})E_{i}$ for $j=i+1,\dots, n$. ### {.unnumbered .unlisted} ![](ForFigs/LUB_new2C.png){width="100%"} ### {.unnumbered .unlisted} - Final matrix with zeros below the diagonal: ![](ForFigs/DM4.svg){width="100%"} ### Back to the i-th step {.unnumbered .unlisted} ![](ForFigs/LUB_new2B.png){width="50%"} - $E_{j}\to E_{j}-(a_{ji}^{(i)}/a_{ii}^{(i)})E_{i}$ for $j=i+1,\dots, n$. - $\underbrace{\underbrace{\Big[\overbrace{0,\dots,0}^{i-1} ,a_{ji}^{(i)},a_{j,i+1}^{(i)},\dots a_{j,n+1}^{(i)}\Big]}_{E_{j}}-(a_{ji}^{(i)}/a_{ii}^{(i)})\underbrace{\Big[\overbrace{0,\dots,0}^{i-1} ,a_{ii}^{(i)},a_{i,i+1}^{(i)},\dots a_{i,n+1}^{(i)}\Big]}_{E_{i}}}_{new~E_{j}}$ ### Update at $i$-th step (new $E_{j}$) {.unnumbered .unlisted} $$ \begin{align*} \Big[\overbrace{0,\dots,0}^{i-1} ,a_{ji}^{(i)},a_{j,i+1}^{(i)},\dots a_{j,n+1}^{(i)}\Big]-(a_{ji}^{(i)}/a_{ii}^{(i)})\Big[\overbrace{0,\dots,0}^{i-1} ,a_{ii}^{(i)},a_{i,i+1}^{(i)},\dots a_{i,n+1}^{(i)}\Big]&\\ =\blue{\Big[\overbrace{0,\dots,0}^{i-1},\overbrace{a_{j,i}^{(i+1)}}^{=0} ,a_{j,i+1}^{(i+1)},a_{j,i+2}^{(i+1)},\dots a_{j,n+1}^{(i+1)}\Big]} \end{align*} $$ $$\blue{a_{jk}^{(i+1)}}\black{=a_{jk}^{(i)}-}\red{(a_{ji}^{(i)}/a_{ii}^{(i)})}\black{a_{ik}^{(i)}\qquad k=i+1,\dots, n+1}\nonumber$$ ::: columns ::: {.column .extrapad width="40%"} - Define multiplier: - $\red{m_{ji}=(a_{ji}^{(i)}/a_{ii}^{(i)})}$ - Thus: $\blue{a_{jk}^{(i+1)}}\black{=a_{jk}^{(i)}-}\red{m_{ji}}\black{a_{ik}^{(i)}}$ - $k=i+1,\dots, n+1\nonumber$ ::: ::: {.column width="60%"} ![](ForFigs/LUB_new2C.png){width="70%"} ::: ::: ### Pseudocode {.unnumbered .unlisted .scrollable} Multipliers: $\red{m_{ji}=(a_{ji}^{(i)}/a_{ii}^{(i)})}$. Update: $\blue{a_{jk}^{(i+1)}}\black{=a_{jk}^{(i)}-}\red{m_{ji}}\black{a_{ik}^{(i)}}$ with $k=i+1,\dots, n+1\nonumber$ ::: columns ::: {.column width="60%"} ``` pseudocode #| label: alg-Gauss0 #| html-indent-size: "1.2em" #| html-comment-delimiter: "//" #| html-line-number: true #| html-line-number-punc: ":" #| html-no-end: false #| pdf-placement: "!htb" #| pdf-line-number: true \begin{algorithm} \caption{Naive Gaussian Elimination} \begin{algorithmic} \State \textbf{Input:} $n\times n$ matrix $A$. $n\times 1$ vector $b$. \State \textbf{Output:} $\tilde{A}$ in reduced form. \Procedure{NaiveGaussianElimination}{$A$,$b$} \State Define augmented matrix $\tilde{A}=[A\, \vert \, b]$. \State Denote by $a_{ij}$ the entries of $\tilde{A}$. \For{$i = 1$ \To $n - 1$} \For{$j = i+1$ \To $n$} \State Compute $m_{ji}=a_{ji}/a_{ii}$ \State Set $a_{ji}=0$ \For{$k = i+1$ \To $n+1$} \State $a_{jk}$= $a_{jk}-m_{ji}a_{ik}$ \EndFor \EndFor \EndFor \EndProcedure \end{algorithmic} \end{algorithm} ``` ::: ::: {.column width="40%"} ![](ForFigs/LUB_new2B.png){width="70%"} ![](ForFigs/LUB_new2C.png){width="80%"} ::: ::: - At each step of Gaussian elimination we re-write $\tilde{A}$ i.e. we do not store intermediate matrices $\tilde{A}^{(1)},\dots\tilde{A}^{(n)}$.
**Memory requirements:** same as the inputs. - [**NOTE**]{.alert} This **NAIVE** algorithm is not well defined if the pivot is zero, i.e. $a_{ii}^{(i)}=0$. ### Python Code {.unnumbered .unlisted .scrollable} ::: {#f7c7d88b .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} import numpy as np def naive_gaussian_elimination(A,b): """ Returns numpy array with the augmented matrix tildeA obtained by Gaussian elimination starting from the augmented matrix [A b]. Parameters (Inputs) ---------- A : numpy.ndarray of shape (n,n) Array representing the square matrix A. b : numpy.ndarray of shape (n,1) Array representing the column vector b. Returns ------- tildeA : numpy.ndarray of shape (n,n+1) Array representing the augmented matrix tildeA. """ # Create the initial augmented matrix tildeA=np.hstack((A,b)) #number of rows: n = np.shape(tildeA)[0] # Start Gaussian Elimination for i in range(n-1): for j in range(i+1,n): #compute multiplier m = tildeA[j,i] / tildeA[i,i] #make zeros below pivot tildeA[j,i] = 0 #update jth row (only the non-zero elements) for k in range(i+1,n+1): tildeA[j,k] = tildeA[j,k] - m * tildeA[i,k] return tildeA if __name__ == "__main__": A=np.array([[2,3,4],[4,9,10],[6,15,20]],dtype=float) b=np.array([[2],[6],[12]],dtype=float) tildeA= naive_gaussian_elimination(A, b) print(f"Reduced A=\n{tildeA}") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Reduced A= [[2. 3. 4. 2.] [0. 3. 2. 2.] [0. 0. 4. 2.]] ``` ::: ::: ![](ForFigs/DM1.svg){fig-alt="Performing row operations." fig-align="left" width="100%"}
